usageContext: address and filler among friends: '¿qué onda, güey?', 'no manches, güey'. Often spelled 'wey'. Between close friends it's punctuation; toward a stranger it's rude.
warningNotes: Fine with friends your age; never with elders, bosses, or strangers. Overuse it and you sound like a teenager — which may be the goal.

natural: no way! / you're kidding! / come on!
usageContext: surprise, disbelief, mild protest: '¿te subieron la renta? ¡No manches!'. The euphemism for the vulgar '¡no mames!'.
'está bien chido' = it's really cool; '¡qué chido!' = how cool! Interchangeable with 'padre'.
'¡qué padre!' = how cool!; 'padrísimo' = super cool. Uniquely Mexican use of 'padre' as an adjective.
'la neta' = honestly/to be real; '¿es neta?' = for real?; '¡neta!' = seriously!. A whole conversational stance in one word.

examples.text: La neta, no me gustó la película.
examples.translation: Honestly, I didn't like the movie.

usageContext: 'tengo mucha chamba' = I have a lot of work; 'chambear' = to work; 'buscar chamba' = job-hunt.

examples.text: No puedo, tengo mucha chamba hoy.
examples.translation: I can't, I have a lot of work today.

usageContext: 'no traigo lana' = I'm broke / no cash on me; 'costó harta lana' = it cost a lot. Synonyms: varo, feria.

examples.translation: I don't have any cash on me.

usageContext: 'un chavo' = a young guy; 'los chavos' = the kids/young folks. 'Chavo banda' = street kid. Neutral and friendly.

examples.text: Es un chavo muy buena onda.
examples.translation: He's a really nice young guy.

natural: cool / nice / good vibe (person or thing)
usageContext: 'es buena onda' = he/she is cool/nice; 'qué buena onda' = how nice. Opposite 'mala onda' (not cool / a jerk move).

examples.text: Tu jefe es muy buena onda.

natural: right now / in a bit / eventually (elastic)
whoUses: absolutely everyone — the most Mexican word of all
usageContext: famously elastic: 'ahorita voy' can mean in 2 minutes or in 2 hours (or never). 'Ahorita' can even soften a 'no': '¿quieres más? — ahorita no' = not right now.
warningNotes: Don't take it literally as 'right now' — tone and context set the real timeframe. Understanding this is understanding Mexico.

examples.translation: Almost ready? — In just a sec (…ish).

usageContext: 'está muy chafa' = it's really cheap/shoddy; 'un celular chafa' = a junky phone. About quality, not price (that's 'barato').

examples.text: No compres esa marca, está bien chafa.
examples.translation: Don't buy that brand, it's really junky.

usageContext: 'ando bien crudo' = I'm super hungover; 'la cruda' = the hangover. 'Cura la cruda' with menudo or a michelada.

examples.text: Hoy no salgo, ando bien crudo.
examples.translation: I'm not going out today, I'm really hungover.

usageContext: describes an affluent, mannered, sometimes snobby style of person or speech: 'habla muy fresa', 'es bien fresa'. Can be mild teasing or a real dig.
warningNotes: Judgmental about class/attitude — fine to recognize, easy to misfire if you use it about someone.

natural: what a drag / I can't be bothered
whoUses: everyone informally, though a touch crude
usageContext: 'me da hueva' = I can't be bothered / it's a drag; '¡qué hueva!' = ugh, so lazy-making. Mildly vulgar (related to 'huevos') but very common.
warningNotes: 'Flojera' is the clean equivalent for polite company.

examples.text: Me da hueva ir al gimnasio hoy.
examples.translation: I really can't be bothered to go to the gym today.

usageContext: 'aguanta' / 'aguántame tantito' = hold on a sec. Also 'no aguanto' = I can't stand it/take it.

examples.translation: Hold on a sec, I'm coming.

literal: twin / compadre / of the same flesh
whoUses: everyone, with rising closeness: cuate < compa < carnal
usageContext: the friend ladder: 'un cuate' (a buddy), 'mi compa' (my pal), 'mi carnal' (my brother-from-another-mother, tightest). All warm and Mexican.

examples.text: Voy a jugar futbol con unos cuates.
examples.translation: I'm going to play soccer with some buddies.
